mnemonics.title: Permutation Formula Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: Think 'nPr = n! over n-r!' - say it as 'Factorial of n divided by factorial of n minus r' - the formula is in the name itself!
For derangements, remember: !n = round(n!/e). It's the 'subfactorial' - like factorial but with alternating subtraction.
Circular permutations: (n-1)! - think 'one less' because rotations are considered the same. Fix one position and arrange the rest.

acronyms.expansion: n Permute r - arrangements of n things taken r at a time where order matters
acronyms.usage: Use when the order/sequence of selection is important (arranging books on shelf, ranking people)

acronyms.expansion: n Choose r - selection of r items from n where order doesn't matter
acronyms.usage: Use when only the group matters, not arrangement (forming committees, selecting items)

acronyms.expansion: Subfactorial n - derangements of n objects (no element in original position)
acronyms.usage: Use for counting permutations where nothing stays in place (rearranging books so none returns to original position)

visualTips.visualization: Imagine arranging 3 books on a shelf (order matters = permutation) vs selecting 3 books to take on a trip (order doesn't matter = combination). Draw boxes representing positions vs circles representing selections.
Picture a round table with name cards. Rotating the entire arrangement doesn't create a new seating. Fix one person (anchor) and arrange others around them. That's why (n-1)! not n!.
Think of shuffling a deck of cards where no card stays in its original position. Use inclusion-exclusion visualization - total permutations minus those with at least one fixed point.

analogies.analogy: Like seating arrangements in a theater - who sits where matters, just like the order of objects in a permutation.
Like forming a cricket team - the team members matter, not who sits where. The group composition is what counts.
Like trying to return borrowed books to wrong owners - none gets their original book back.
